---
title: "Appendix: Matrix Calculus for the Normal Equations"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 1 · Lecture B — supplement"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

## Where we are {.smaller}

Lecture B stated the gradient of the least-squares loss directly:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = -\tfrac{2}{N}\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}).
$$

This appendix fills in the two vector-calculus identities behind it and turns the
crank to $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = (\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}$.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: optional depth for students who want the derivative mechanics. Two
identities, one expansion, one differentiation — that is the whole story.
:::

## Two identities {.smaller}

For a constant vector $\mathbf{a}$ and constant matrix $\mathbf{A}$:

::: {.fragment}
$$
\frac{\partial\,(\mathbf{a}^{\!\top}\mathbf{w})}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{a}
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
$$
\frac{\partial\,(\mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w})}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\!\top})\,\mathbf{w}
\;=\; 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w} \quad(\mathbf{A}\ \text{symmetric}).
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the linear term differentiates to its coefficient vector; the
quadratic form to $(\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{A}^{\!\top})\mathbf{w}$. $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$
is symmetric, so its quadratic form gives the clean $2\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}$.
:::

## Expand the loss {.smaller}

Write the loss as a quadratic in $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \tfrac{1}{N}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w})^{\!\top}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w})
$$

::: {.fragment}
Multiply out — the two cross terms are equal scalars ($\mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}$):

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \tfrac{1}{N}\bigl(\mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\bigr)
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: a constant, a linear term, and a quadratic term — exactly the two
shapes the identities handle.
:::

## Differentiate & solve {.smaller}

Apply the two identities term by term ($\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}$,
$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ symmetric):

::: {.fragment}
$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{N}\bigl(-2\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} + 2\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\bigr)
= -\tfrac{2}{N}\,\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w})
$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
Set it to zero — the $-2/N$ drops out:

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\,\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}\;}
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this is the gradient Lecture B used, now earned. The "$\nabla=0 \Rightarrow$
normal equations" step is the P1-M1 checkpoint target.
:::
